one_line_takeaway: Adversarial bug reports are a cheap, effective attack on LLM-based APR: crafted issues coerced SWE-agent into attacker-aligned patches 90% of the time while the best single pre-filter caught only 47% and CodeQL caught none, exposing a ~1000x attacker/defender cost asymmetry.
key_finding: SWE-agent generated a pull request for all 51 adversarial issues, and 46 (90.2%) produced patches matching the attacker's intended goal. By category: all 8 Deceptive Noise and 5/5 Naive APR wasted resources; all 10 CI/CD attacks introduced secret-leak/RCE-capable logic; 10/14 Injection attacks embedded attacker-controlled logic; 13/14 Revert-CVE attacks reintroduced previously fixed vulnerabilities. Defenses were weak: the single best pre-APR filter (o4-mini custom prompt with UNstructured output) flagged only 24/51 (47%); CodeQL detected zero malicious patches; Copilot surfaced security-relevant comments for 30/51 (58.8%) but only across all confidence levels including hidden low-confidence/file-level comments requiring manual triage (only 1 high-confidence security comment). Test suites caught only 12/51 (23.5%) outright. The best composite defense (best pre-filter + post-APR Copilot ensemble) blocked 35/51 (68.6%); the full pre-APR ensemble + post ensemble reached 37/51 (72.5%) but at high false-positive cost. A stark cost asymmetry underpins the threat: generating an adversarial report costs ~1e-4 USD, pre-APR filtering ~1e-3 USD/issue, and APR execution ~1e0 USD/issue (mean $0.87/issue) - a roughly three-order-of-magnitude imbalance favoring attackers.
core_contribution: The paper (MSR '26) identifies and empirically demonstrates a novel attack surface in LLM-based Automated Program Repair (APR): adversarial bug reports. Because LLM-driven APR systems (e.g., SWE-agent) ingest untrusted natural-language issue reports and automatically synthesize + test patches, an attacker who submits realistic-looking but malicious issues can coerce the system into producing insecure or harmful code changes. The authors' own contributions are: (1) a formal STRIDE-based threat model of how bug-reporting interfaces can be used to subvert APR; (2) an open-source prototype that automates generation and evaluation of adversarial bug reports (using a cheap, locally-run DevStral:24B model plus LSH/rapidfuzz context-building from CVE-fix commits); and (3) a systematic evaluation of pre-APR and post-APR defenses. They generate 51 adversarial bug reports across five tactics and run them end-to-end against a real APR pipeline with commercial-grade guards.
threat_model: Adversary submits high-quality, LLM-generated bug reports at scale through the public issue-submission interface (authentication is assumed ineffective since fake identities are cheap; no model poisoning and no source-code access strictly required, though source access helps). Protected assets: source-code integrity (no backdoors / reintroduced CVEs), the CI/CD environment (must resist misuse during reproduction/testing), and operational resources (reviewer time + compute). Adversary goals: introduce vulnerabilities, leak secrets, or waste resources. Attack surface spans the entry point (issue interface), the internal execution path (reproduction/synthesis/validation), and impact targets (production codebase, CI/CD). STRIDE-mapped threats emphasized: Tampering (malicious code insertion via APR), Information Disclosure (CI/CD-time secret leaks), Denial of Service (compute/review overload), and Elevation of Privilege (via CI or inserted code); Spoofing and Repudiation are deemed less relevant in open bug reporting.
limitations: Authors' stated threats to validity: (Internal) manual annotation relies on expert judgment despite a dual-annotator protocol; LLM non-determinism could not be eliminated because they could not fix temperature to 0 without harming construct validity (deviating from default APR/defense settings). (External) results cover only five open-source Python projects and a single APR system (SWE-agent), so generalization to proprietary software or differently-architected APR tools is uncertain - though they argue the exploited structural properties (unvetted NL input, LLM patch synthesis, test-based validation) are shared by SWE-agent/OpenHands/AutoCodeRover/RepairAgent. (Construct) attack success is defined via patch generation, detection bypass, and presence of malicious behavior, which may not capture downstream exploitation feasibility or user-facing harm. (Tool) reliance on specific versions of LLMs, CodeQL, and Copilot may affect replicability. Additional reviewer notes: the study is a demonstration/red-teaming exercise (51 issues) run on private mirrors, not a large-scale statistical study; the abstract's own '58%' post-repair figure is Copilot's aggregate including hidden comments, and CodeQL's contribution was 0%. Some attack classes (Naive APR, obvious CI/CD/Injection payloads like 'extract full environment to external server') were judged easy for humans to spot, so the more insidious real risks are the Noise-APR and Revert-CVE classes.

datasets_used: 5 SWE-bench Python repositories: django, flask, pip, requests, scikit-learn
51 self-generated adversarial bug reports (5 Naive APR, 8 Deceptive Noise, 10 CI/CD exploits, 14 code injection, 14 Revert-CVE)
100 real psf/requests issues (#6000-6100, one deleted) used for the false-positive study
quantitative_results: 51/51 adversarial issues produced a pull request from SWE-agent
46/51 (90.2%) patches achieved the attacker's intended malicious outcome
Best single pre-APR filter (o4-mini, unstructured output) detected 24/51 = 47.05%
PromptGuard family detected 3/51 (5.88%); LlamaGuard family 0; Granite-Guardian 5/51 (9.80%)
Copilot surfaced security-relevant comments for 30/51 (58.8%) across all confidence levels (17 low-confidence 33%, 27 file-level 52.9%, only 1 high-confidence)
Test suites: 12/51 (23.5%) failed outright, 4/51 (7.8%) increased within flaky threshold, 35/51 (68.6%) no new failures
Best composite defense (best pre-filter + post ensemble): 35/51 (68.6%) blocked; full pre+post ensemble: 37/51 (72.5%)
Cost asymmetry: attack ~$0.000295/report (total $0.0133), pre-APR filter ~$0.00268/issue (o4-mini), APR ~$0.87/issue; ~1e-4 << 1e-3 << 1e0 USD
False-positive study: o4-mini unstructured 3/100 FP; GPT-4.1-mini unstructured 34/100 FP (prohibitive); structured output near-zero FP
baselines_compared: Pre-APR guards: Meta PromptGuard & PromptGuardV2 (98M), Meta LlamaGuard v3 & v4, IBM Granite-Guardian
Pre-APR custom prompts: OpenAI GPT-4.1-mini and o4-mini, each in structured-JSON and plain-text variants
Post-APR detectors: GitHub CodeQL (Python ruleset) and GitHub Copilot code review
APR system under test: SWE-agent v1.1.0 (SW-ReX v1.2.2) with Claude Sonnet claude-sonnet-4-20250514
